campaigns.title: Help visually impaired Shivaprakash give 40 blind girls shelter, education and care
campaigns.description: At the age of 8, Shivaprakash lost his vision. But what could have become a life of despair became something else entirely. Shivaprakash went on to complete his Master’s degree and eventually founded the Divya Jyothi Charitable Trust, an organisation born out of personal struggle and driven by a powerful mission, to educate and empower blind girls, who are among the most neglected and marginalised in society.In many families, blind daughters are hidden away, seen as a burden, and in some cases, even as a curse. They are denied the right to dream, grow, or be independent. At Divya Jyothi, Shivaprakash is changing that narrative. Your support can ensure that no blind girl is left behind simply because of her disability. Donate today to help them stand tall, proud, and self-reliant. Donate nowDonate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).Education is survivalIn communities where gender and disability intersect, blind girls face deep-rooted prejudice and extreme isolation. Many are denied even basic schooling and are left dependent on family members for the rest of their lives. Recognising this, Divya Jyothi began with a simple yet radical step: to provide blind girls with computer training so they could access the digital world and open doors to employment.But the journey didn’t stop there. The NGO went on to launch a braille transcription centre, ensuring girls have access to learning materials that suit their needs. As Shivaprakash says, “Without education, no one can do anything. That's why education is even more important for blind girls.” Education isn’t just about literacy, it’s about self-worth, decision-making, mobility, and freedom. It’s about giving these girls the same right to participate in society that sighted individuals often take for granted.From rejection to respectIn a country where blind girls are often treated as liabilities, Divya Jyothi is rewriting their futures. Over 1,000 girls have graduated from the Trust’s training centres. Many of them come from families that had almost given up on them. Today, these same girls are government employees, corporate professionals, and role models in their communities.More than 200 graduates have secured jobs in the public sector. Over 300 are now employed in private companies. Every success story is proof that with the right support, blind girls don’t just survive, they thrive. “Most girls are neglected by their families. This is a major challenge for them,” says Shivaprakash. By providing them with skills and confidence, Divya Jyothi is turning neglect into empowerment and helplessness into hope.Donate to help 40 blind girls find independenceAlmost 40 girls are currently enrolled and will directly benefit from your support. These are girls with potential, courage, and ambition—but they lack the resources to pursue an independent life. “I request each one to come forward and donate to help blind girls lead independent lives in society,” urges Shivaprakash.Help Divya Jyothi Charitable Trust break the cycle of neglect. Your donation can give a blind girl her first lesson, her first job, and a life of dignity.Donate to this fundraiser by Give, India’s most trusted online donation platform, to support Divya Jyothi Charitable Trust in its mission to shelter and educate neglected and abandoned blind girls.

campaigns.title: Help Prasad provide shelter and care to orphaned kids and abandoned elderly parents
campaigns.description: Prasad and his wife Anu once had a daughter, little Prarthana , who passed away when she was still a toddler. Heartbroken but determined to honour her memory, they decided to dedicate their lives to those who had no one.  What began as adopting eight distressed children has now grown into a loving community that survives solely because Prasad refuses to give up. “My father, a farmer, ended his life owing to debt and distress. What I have faced as a child, I don't want any other child to face the same, and that is the biggest motivation. At present, we have 45 children and 25 abandoned elderly in our shelter home. We are their family. Anu (my wife) loves them like her own, and I provide for them and protect them like any father would,” says Prasad who stands as the last hope for these young ones and the elderly.  Unfortunately, he has been supporting this home with his meager earnings as an autorickshaw driver. He is in urgent need of help to keep a roof over his large family. You can lend him a hand in his noble work. Donate as much as you can today.Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).When pain became a missionPrasad knows what it means to grow up with hunger, fear, and the constant uncertainty of survival. He remembers the nights his family slept without food, the days his mother struggled alone after his father’s suicide, and the years he worked as a child doing menial jobs just to stay afloat. Today, those memories shape every decision he makes as the founder of Prathana Foundation, a home where abandoned elderly parents and orphaned children live together, as one family.“Our home is very unique. Here, orphans and elderly parents who have been abandoned by their children live together as a family. Here grandparents get the love of grandchildren, and orphaned kids are cared for by the elderly,” he shares with quiet pride.  In this one home, 45 children and 25 abandoned elderly have found love, belonging, and dignity, things life once denied them.But behind this warmth is a daily struggle that Prasad carries alone.  The daily struggle for survival“It’s very difficult for me to earn enough to sustain such a big home as an auto rickshaw driver. I am constantly in fear that if I run out of money, what will happen to the elderly and the orphans,” he says. The rising number of children and elderly people taking shelter here means more mouths to feed, more medicines to buy, more hospital visits, and more responsibilities on a man who already gives everything he has.Even after both he and Anu lost their jobs, he took up auto-rickshaw driving so the home could run. But the expenses of running such a large shelter are overwhelming.  Children need nutritious rations, school supplies, a safe environment, and emotional support. Elderly parents, many frail and chronically ill, need constant medical attention, medicines, and hospital visits. And the need keeps growing.I request you all to see these children and elderly as a member of your family and donate generously for them, so that their lives can be transformed,” Prasad appeals.Your support today can ensure- Daily nutritious meals for children and elderly parents  - Medical care, medicines, and emergency support for the elderly  - Safe shelter, clothing, and hygiene essentials  - Schooling, books, and basic education for every child  In a world where abandonment and loneliness break so many lives, Prathana Foundation stands as a rare home where forgotten grandparents and orphaned children rebuild hope together.  But this home, this family, cannot survive without you.  Your contribution can help Prasad continue saying “yes” to every abandoned child… every lonely elderly parent… every life that deserves love.  Donate today and help ensure that no elderly mother sleeps alone, no child suffers the scars Prasad once carried, and no family built on love collapses because of financial struggle.  Donate to this fundraiser on give.do, India’s most trusted online donation platform, to support Prathana Foundation in its mission to rescue and care for orphans of farmers and abandoned elderly. Your kindness can help keep this beautiful family together.

campaigns.title: Help Mansi bring hope into lives of children fighting for life, Donate now
campaigns.description: When even hospitals and doctors gave up on children fighting for life Mansi Shah stood by them. Mansi founded Happy Feet Home, an NGO that cares for children fighting severe illnesses like thalassemia, HIV, Cancer, etc. Despite being discouraged repeatedly, she refused to turn away from children who were often overlooked simply because their journeys were difficult and uncertain. Today, Happy Feet Home provides children with medical care, nutritious food, education, emotional support and, above all, the love and dignity every child deserves.Your donation can help ensure these children continue receiving lifelong care. Donate today and give them the chance to live with hope.Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking.Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).Born from one unforgettable momentHappy Feet Home was born from an experience Mansi could never forget. "I was very attached to this 3-year-old girl. She had a brain tumour," she says. When the child hugged her tightly before boarding a train home, it felt like a final goodbye. That heartbreaking moment inspired Mansi to dedicate her life to children who may never receive a cure. "If I start something, I will work with children who have illnesses which may not have a cure... That's when the idea of Happy Feet Home came into my mind."Every donation changes a child's futureMany children arrive at Happy Feet Home carrying far more than an illness. "It's not 1, not 2, but 3 or 4 issues that they are grappling with in life," Mansi explains. Poverty, abandonment and years without affection often leave emotional scars as deep as their medical conditions. Some children have even told her that the first hug they ever received was after arriving at the centre.Yet with proper nutrition, medicines, counselling and constant care, lives begin to change. One girl who was once not responding to treatment is now a healthy college student. Another boy arrived weighing just 17 kilos after living on the streets. Today, he is healthy and learning professional cricket. These transformations are only possible because compassionate people chose to donate.Why your support is urgently neededProviding lifelong care for children with HIV, thalassemia and cancer requires constant support. There was even a time when Happy Feet Home had to shut its centre for nearly two weeks because of a lack of funding. During that period, children lost access to nutrition, education and mental health support. As Mansi explains, "Illnesses like HIV and Thalassemia are incurable. The need for medicine, for treatment, for care and support is going to be a forever need."That is why your donation matters today.Donate to Happy Feet HomeEvery donation helps provide medicines, nutritious meals, education, counselling and a loving home for children fighting serious illnesses. As Mansi beautifully puts it, "As a donor, you are not only giving money. You are ensuring that children with illnesses have the right to live as children. You are giving them hope."If you believe no child should be denied love, dignity or happiness because of illness, please support this NGO for children today. "If you believe that children with HIV, Thalassemia, Cancer have a right to live a dignified life please support our cause and donate today."Donate to this fundraiser by Give, India’s most trusted online donation platform, to support Happy Feet Home in its mission to provide care, dignity, medical support, and hope to children living with life-limiting and life-shortening illnesses.

campaigns.title: Sanjivani- mother to 70 kids rescued from red-light areas, needs help to bring more to safety and care for them
campaigns.description: Renu grew up in the chawls of Pune’s red-light area in a dingy house, witnessing a flow of unknown men enter and leave their small bedroom day and night. Sometimes while looking at her mother hiding her fresh bruises with her pallu, Renu’s determination not to take up this path that her mother was pushed into only became stronger. She yearned for a way out of this dark world where she could live without fear, not struggle for three meals a day and get an education.Luckily for Renu (name changed), Swadhar IDWC-Pune took her in with her mother’s consent and put her in school. After years of hard work, she cleared all her grades and has now appeared for her Class 12 final exams.Swadhar IDWC led by Sanjivani Hingne, is on a mission to bring many more children to safety and your support can make it possible. Donate generously.Donate easily through UPI, all debit/credit cards and net banking. Charitable donations to this fundraising campaign are tax exempted under 80G of the Income Tax Act (India).A fight for the rights of childrenChildren like Renu, who are born to sex workers, cannot dream of a normal childhood. They are subjected to stress, trauma, and sometimes abuse by their mother’s clients. “What will the child get whose mother is a sex worker? There is no family; there is no schooling, no medical care, and nothing for the child. Children like them are easy prey. Our only concern is the child getting into mainstream society and not continuing in the mother’s profession,” says Sanjivani Hingne, secretary, Swadhar IDWC Pune.Swadhar IDWC- Pune weaves a safety net for children of sex workers. They strive to prevent second-generation prostitution of children living in red-light areas by providing a secure environment where they have access to shelter, nutrition, healthcare, education, and happiness. The organisation wants to provide a refuge for more children trapped in the sex-trade environment, but they cannot do it without your support.Project Mohor - a safe home away from red-light areasSwadhar’s fully functional 24x7 facility looks after the most vulnerable and neglected children of sex workers in Pune’s red-light area. These children often roam the streets till early morning while their mothers engage in prostitution. In view of providing a safe environment for them, Swadhar runs a kitchen, a pre-school, and an e-learning center.“Right from cleaning, bathing, feeding, medical care, seeing that the child is ready for school - all of it is taken care of here, which is impossible in a red-light area. So far, we have taken care of 200 children, most of them now pursuing higher education or have finished school with flying colours. Now, we need more funds because the number of children is increasing and we want to take in more children”.Unfortunately, this safe home for kids is at risk of shutting down. The NGO is racing against time to raise funds to build a new home for the rescued kids and your help can strengthen their efforts. Please donate as much as you can.Donate to this fundraiser on Give.do, India’s largest and most trusted online donation platform, to help minor victims of forced prostitution get back to school and reshape their lives.

campaigns.title: Let's not forget them!
campaigns.description: ARE WE FORGETTING THEM?  When we think of childhood, most of us recall happy memories—innocence, the unconditional love of parents, and a carefree life filled with joy. If you were fortunate enough to have such a childhood, you are truly blessed.But around us are children whose childhoods look very different—marked by hardship, stress, and loneliness. Some have no parents, while others have parents unable to care for them. Some are drawn into crime at a tender age, while others face exploitation far too young. Many live in children’s homes or orphanages, while some survive without any protection—on the streets, under flyovers, or along riverbanks.For these children, BELIEF runs KITABGHAR—a safe, brave, and non‑judgmental space where books become companions and pathways to hope.Take Payal (name changed), who lives by the river. Her parents work as daily laborers. Though bright and enrolled in school, she lacks the supportive environment needed to learn. Her days are spent doing odd jobs, eating donated food, catching fish, or sometimes begging.Or Hussain (name changed), who lives nearby with his siblings under the care of their elderly grandmother. His father and uncle have passed away, leaving no one else in the family. Like Payal, he too works whatever jobs he can find. Poverty and instability have driven many adults in their community to addiction, and our team worries the children may follow the same path.Are we forgetting the children in our own city?This question gave birth to KITABGHAR.Here, children gather around books. They listen to stories, join activities, talk, and connect with the wider world through reading. They find space to reflect on their dreams, feelings, and aspirations. For children living on the streets or riverbanks, we also hold classes in reading, writing, and basic arithmetic.Even children unintentionally caught up in crime attend Kitabghar sessions. Because our facilitators work with a non‑judgmental approach, these children have begun to trust Kitabghar. Books they had never touched before—storybooks, even textbooks—now spark their interest. They are discovering joy in reading and art.IMPACT: Today, Kitabghar runs 13 sessions every week across Nashik and Pune, reaching about 200 children. Over the past two years, we have conducted nearly 400 sessions. The impact is visible: children who once couldn’t even sit still and pay attention now stay engaged for entire sessions. They recognize authors, solve word puzzles, recite poems, compose their own verses, create stories, and even stage plays. Those who couldn’t write are now trying, supported by peers and facilitators. Older children are joining discussions, expressing opinions with confidence, and learning to listen and reflect on others’ views.YOUR SUPPORT MATTERS: Kitabghar is entirely community‑funded, and we urgently need financial help to continue.CONTRIBUTIONS WILL BE USED FOR:Carefully chosen, high‑quality books for childrenStationery and art suppliesNutritious snacks for children living on the streetsModest honorariums and travel costs for facilitatorsCost of support to 1 center for 1 month = 18000Cost of support to 1 child for 1 year = 6000Cost of a single session = 2300Please extend your support to brighten both the present and the future of these children.Pledge your support, donate now! Let's not forget them!

campaigns.title: VISHWA JYOTHI OLD AGE HOME
campaigns.description: At Vishwa Jyothi NGO, we provide food and shelter for elderly people who are not taken care of by their families because of various reasons. We understand the difficulties that the elderly face, especially when they are living in poverty. Our Old Age Home provides daily needs for the elderly and ensures they are in a comfortable environment. With the support of Vishwa Jyothi NGO, we are looking to upgrade our Old Age Home and take it to the next level. Our elderly residents are enthusiastic about the new plans and goals we have set for them. We have also planned creative activities such as Bag making, Incense making, Candle making, etc. to develop their financial status through skill development activities. This will not only improve their economic status but also help them become more self-reliant.We believe that through these activities, the elderly can improve their family background and economy. We aim to provide training on creative activities and awareness camps about self-dependency to make them more self-sufficient. With this initiative, the elderly at our Old Age Home will have the opportunity to earn some pocket money for their daily needs and also contribute to their families.We help women like Saraswati. She was born with a disability and was both homeless and parentless. She used to live on the roadside and beg for her livelihood. But now, at 75 years old, she cannot take care of herself. That's why I brought her to our caring centre, Vishwa Jyothi old age home.

campaigns.title: Donate a Sewing Machine to Poor Woman & Tailoring Training
campaigns.description: This project will train 150 poor women and teach them valuable sewing skills. It will also provide each woman with her own treadle sewing machine, allowing her to earn a living from home to support her family.Challenge: To a struggling woman or girl in countries like India, the sewing machine you provide can be the doorway to a better life. You’ll help a single mother, orphaned teen, or widow feel the dignity of providing for herself and her family. In India, more than 50 per cent of women are illiterate. Living in poverty with a limited education greatly obstructs these women from finding quality jobs to support their families. They are often at risk of being trapped in dehumanizing professions (like the sex trade) in order to feed their children. This project will empower poor women in rural villages with valuable sewing skills and a sewing machine so they can earn a respectable living and help their families.Solution:  The women will attend a sewing machine tailoring centre and get 6 months of training, where they will develop their sewing skills and learn about basic business practices. Once a woman graduates from the tailoring program, she will receive her own treadle (foot powered) branded sewing machine. She will be able to start her own tailoring business from home and earn a daily income that can provide food and basic necessities for the whole family.Long-Term Impact: The project will empower 150 women each year with sewing skills and provide them with a sewing machine to start their own self-sustaining tailoring business. These women, who are often poor mothers and widows, will have an opportunity to use their skills to rise out of poverty and work toward a promising future for themselves and their families.#womenempowerment #sewingmachinedonation #donateonline #donatecharity #charitydonation #sponsorfood #fooddonation #donatingmoney #ngodonation #donatengo #girlchilddonation #sponsorsewingmachine #supportpoorpeople #indiahelpaged #donatepoor #giveindadonation #donatefood #givedonation #donategiveindia #givefoundation #giveonline #covid-19 #coronavirus #pandemic #donatecorona #taxexemption #80gdonation #donatingcharity #caronavirusoutbreak #CoronaCombatForce #secondwave #coronasecondwave #poorpeopledonation #bestcharitydonation #donateforpoor #usddonation #moneydonation #tailoringtrainingforwomen #skilltrainingdonation #donatecare #giveindiadonations #globalgiving #CharityforPoor #FeedTheNeedy #MilaapDonation #HelpPoor #donatemoney #onlinedonation #80gtaxexemption

campaigns.title: Help abandoned elderly with shelter and care
campaigns.description: Every day, elderly parents are left alone on the streets, at train stations, or even outside temples. Many are sick, struggling with age-related problems, and have no money or family to turn to. Some suffer for weeks or months before anyone notices their pain. The heartbreaking truth? They were abandoned by their own families.Although Indian law requires children and relatives to care for senior citizens, many elders still face neglect, abuse, and abandonment. The problem is only getting worse.But there is hope. Our dedicated NGO partners work day and night to rescue these forgotten elders. They provide safe shelter, medical care, nutritious meals, and most importantly—love and respect. Through old age homes across the country, our partners give these seniors a chance to live their remaining years with dignity, comfort, and peace.You can make a difference. Your donation will help our NGO partners:Rescue homeless elders from the streetsProvide shelter, food, and medical careRestore their faith in humanity with kindness and supportNo elder should spend their final years alone and suffering. Your generosity can give them a fresh start. Donate today and help an abandoned senior citizen find safety, care, and hope.

To help me create more impact and touch more lives, I need your support and contribution. Please back my fundraiser so that together we can build a better India for all of us. Pledge your support, donate now!

campaigns.title: Help Save Young Girls From Trafficking
campaigns.description: Human trafficking in India is a horrifying crisis. Thousands of victims, primarily women and girl children, are exploited in red-light areas. Over 10,659 cases were reported between 2018-2022, but experts say that these numbers are likely a fraction of the reality due to underreporting, yet they still do expose the magnitude of the crisis. Girls make up 71.4% of missing children in India and a large number of these girls end up at red-light areas. Sex trafficking is particularly gruesome, with girls as young as 12 sold for as little as a few thousand rupees.
